1:
The witnesses open with the question of Joshua's prudence. Matthew Henry observes that Joshua, like a watchful general, sent spies not as Moses had sent them, to survey the whole land (Num. 13:1), but to view Jericho only, and to report to himself alone — careful 'to take the first step well and not to stumble at the threshold.' His maxim governs the whole scene: faith in God's promise ought not to supersede but to encourage our diligence — 'we do not trust God, but tempt him, if our expectations slacken our endeavours.' John Calvin presses the harder question, whether Joshua leaned too much on his own prudence in sending scouts without consulting God; he answers that the commander who would never have moved the camp without God's order most probably sought his pleasure here also, or God himself 'spontaneously suggested it to the mind of his servant.' John Wesley agrees this was no distrust, and reads 'secretly' with a shrewd turn: the secrecy was not toward the enemy, for all spies work so, but toward the Israelites themselves, lest a discouraging report should again dishearten the camp. John Gill notes that Joshua 'had sent' the men from Shittim before the order to decamp, and sets aside the Jewish tradition that they were Caleb and Phinehas: men of such rank were not likely sent, but meaner persons of good sense and probity.

The real division concerns their hostess. Adam Clarke mounts a long defence of Rahab's reputation: the Hebrew zonah, he argues, should be rendered 'tavern-keeper or hostess,' since among the Egyptians and Greeks women kept the houses of entertainment; the Chaldee Targum so translates it; and it is not likely that Salmon, a prince of Judah, would have married a public prostitute, nor that Providence would have suffered such a person into the sacred genealogy of our Lord (Matt. 1:5). Calvin will have none of this: it is 'a regular practice with the Rabbins,' he retorts, 'presumptuously to wrest Scripture' to save a nation's honour; the received meaning of the word stands, and the grace of God is the more striking for it, that it 'could thus penetrate into a place of shame' and draw out of it not only Rahab but her father's whole family. Wesley and Gill likewise keep the plain sense, which the apostles' Greek confirms (Heb. 11:31; Jas. 2:25). Henry mediates: she had formerly been of ill fame and, like Simon the leper, wore the reproach of it in her name after she had repented — proof that the greatness of sin is no bar to pardoning mercy, for publicans and harlots enter the kingdom (Matt. 21:31).

All sides converge on the providence in the lodging. Henry marvels that God's Israel had, for aught that appears, 'but one friend, but one well-wisher in all Jericho, and that was Rahab a harlot'; had the scouts gone to any other house they would have been betrayed, but God knew where they had a friend though they did not, and 'that which seems to us most contingent and accidental is often over-ruled by the divine providence to serve its great ends.' Calvin adds the practical observation that a house of her description, standing in a retired corner upon the city wall, exactly suited men who were courting secrecy. So a verse that begins with military prudence becomes, in the witnesses' hands, the first page of a Canaanite's conversion.
2:
John Gill explains how the alarm reached the throne: the king of Jericho, knowing Israel's claim to Canaan and their design upon it, had employed men to watch and report whatever passed in the city and its environs — or citizens, for their own safety, volunteered the intelligence. Keil and Delitzsch keep close to the letter: strange men had come in 'to-night,' a note the narrative itself sharpens at v.5, as night was falling and the gate about to be shut; the king, suspecting their reason for coming, summoned Rahab to give them up.

John Calvin does most of the interpretive work, and in two directions at once. On the one hand, the denunciation was entirely natural: watchmen are posted in doubtful emergencies; the Israelites were near at hand, had openly told Edom and Moab that they sought a settlement in Canaan, were formidable for numbers, and had just slain two neighbouring kings — 'it would therefore have argued extreme supineness in such manifest danger to allow any strangers whatever to pass freely' through a frontier city. On the other hand, the truly remarkable thing is not that the spies were reported but that they were not caught. With moderate diligence the messengers could easily have been detained at the gates, and a search would certainly have found them; instead the citizens were 'supernaturally blinded,' struck with a 'judicial amazement,' so that 'they acted in everything without method or counsel.' The terror of God had already unhinged Jericho before a single Israelite crossed the Jordan.

Calvin also records and rejects an interpretation that turned the episode against Joshua: some have thought the spies' peril was a punishment of his distrust, since he ought to have boldly passed the Jordan trusting the divine guidance. The result, Calvin replies, leads to the opposite conclusion — by rescuing the messengers from extreme danger God 'gave new courage to the people; for in that manifestation of his power he plainly showed that he was watching over their safety, and providing for their happy entrance into the promised land.' Meanwhile the tension of the scene is not to be smoothed away: the king sends, the men lie hidden, and their life 'hangs upon the tongue of a woman, just as if it were hanging by a thread.' The witnesses read the whole verse, then, as a study in double vision — a vigilant king doing exactly what a threatened king should do, and a sovereign God quietly making all that vigilance useless.
3:
John Gill is careful about how the king knew where to send: not merely because Rahab kept a public house where strangers often lodged, so that the men might be conjectured to be there, but 'upon certain information that they were seen to go in there.' The demand itself, as Keil and Delitzsch render it, is peremptory and, on its own premises, just: 'Bring forth the men that are come to thee... for they be come to search out all the country.' Matthew Henry allows the doomed king this much fairness: 'he had cause to fear when the enemy was at his door, and his fear made him suspicious and jealous of all strangers'; he had reason to demand that Rahab bring forth men who were, in truth, exactly what he suspected — spies.

Adam Clarke finds in the king's procedure another support for his reading of Rahab's calling: had she been a person of ill fame, officers might at once have seized her lodgers as vagabonds; but if she kept a house of entertainment, then by 'the universal custom of the Asiatics' the persons under her roof were sacred and could not be molested on trifling grounds — 'a guest or a friend is sacred in whatever house he may be received, in every part of the east to the present day.' Hence, on Clarke's view, the king must formally require the men from her rather than simply drag them out. The other witnesses do not follow him in the inference, but the observation stands on its own: the narrative shows a summons, not a raid, and the interval it created is precisely what gave Rahab room to act.

John Wesley lifts the moment into the doctrine of providence with a stroke the other witnesses echo: 'Probably Israel had but one friend in all Jericho: and God directed them to her! Thus what seems to be most accidental, is often over-ruled, to serve the great ends of providence. And those that acknowledge God in their ways, he will guide them with his eye.' The spies did not know, when they turned in at Rahab's door, that every other threshold in the city would have been their death; the God who did know had gone before them. The verse thus becomes, for the witnesses together, a small window into the hiddenness of divine government: the king's intelligence network and the travellers' seemingly casual choice of lodging are both moving, without knowing it, along lines drawn from above.
4:
The witnesses first settle the order of events. John Gill and John Wesley both read 'the woman took the two men' as a pluperfect — she had taken and hidden them before the king's messengers arrived, as soon as the rumour spread that Israelite spies were in her house; Adam Clarke supposes she first secreted them in some private corner until she could stow them on the housetop (v.6). Wesley insists the concealment itself 'is justly mentioned as a great and generous act of faith' (Heb. 11:31), for she ventured her life upon a steadfast persuasion of God's word to Israel.

Then comes the question the church has never stopped asking of this verse, and John Calvin states it doubly: was her treachery to her country excusable, and could her lie be free from fault? On the first he is remarkably bold. Love of country is implanted by nature, and it is 'a puerile evasion' to plead that war was not yet declared; what exempts Rahab is that God had made his judgment known to her, so that her adoption into the body of the Church was 'a kind of manumission from the common law by which citizens are bound toward each other.' In order to pass by faith to a new people, she had to renounce her own. On the lie he is equally firm the other way: those who defend the 'dutiful lie' (mendacium officiosum) 'do not sufficiently consider how precious truth is in the sight of God'; it can never be lawful to lie, 'because that cannot be right which is contrary to the nature of God. And God is truth.' Yet — like Rebecca obtaining the blessing for Jacob (Gen. 27) — her act 'is not devoid of the praise of virtue, although it was not spotlessly pure'; God suppressed the fault and did not impute the bad mixed up with the good.

The others fall along the same line with different accents. Wesley is blunt: the answer 'was false, and therefore unquestionably sinful; tho' her intention was good therein' — though as a heathen she might well think an officious lie lawful. Charles Ellicott observes that the falsehood 'evidently left no stain on Rahab's conscience, although all falsehood is sin': the divine standard never varies, but the standard of man's conscience, even where faith is dominant, varies greatly — as with Jael and Sisera. Matthew Henry comes nearest to a defence: had she told the truth or kept silence she would have betrayed the spies; none are bound to accuse their friends of what, 'though enquired after as a crime, they know to be a virtue'; those that might be destroyed might be deceived. Yet even he concedes that divines generally conceive it was a sin, extenuated by her dim views of the divine law, and closes where all the witnesses close: God accepted her faith and pardoned her infirmity, but we must never do evil that good may come (Rom. 3:8).
5:
The source material on this verse is brief, but the witnesses make its details count. John Gill notes the civic routine on which Rahab's story turns: the gate of the city was shut every night 'at a certain time,' and it is to that fixed hour — 'about the time of the shutting of the gate, when it was dark' — that she pins her tale. The detail gives her fiction its plausibility: strangers who wished to be gone before the gate closed would naturally slip out at dusk, and pursuers who believed her had no time to lose. Hence the audacity of her closing words to the officers: pursue after them quickly, for ye shall overtake them — sending the king's men racing out of the very gate behind which the spies lay hidden on her roof.

Adam Clarke draws out the timing on the other side: since Rahab could speak of the gate being shut at dark, it appears that the king's summons itself came after nightfall, and 'the season itself was friendly to the whole plot: had these transactions taken place in daylight, it is scarcely possible that the spies could have escaped.' Darkness covered their entry, darkness confused the search, and darkness would soon cover their descent from the wall. Yet Clarke will not let the favourable circumstances become an argument for the method: 'this is no excuse for the woman's prevarication, for God could have saved his messengers independently of her falsity. God never says to any, Do evil that good may come of it.' The providence that made the night friendly did not need the lie; it merely overruled it.

Taken with the verdicts gathered at v.4, the witnesses' reading of this verse is therefore double. As narrative, they admire its coolness: a woman under royal interrogation, her guests' lives and her own hanging on her composure, constructs in a sentence a story exact enough in its details — the dusk, the closing gate, the fugitives' head start — to empty her house of danger. As morality, they decline to canonize the stratagem: what God accepted in Rahab was the faith that risked everything for his people, not the falsehood in which that faith, still 'a heathen's' and dimly taught, clothed itself. The verse thus stands in Scripture unretouched, a record of what she said and not a warrant for saying it — grace crowning the believer while the belief's rough garment awaits better instruction.
6:
The narrator's aside — she had brought them up to the roof and hidden them among the stalks of flax — draws from the witnesses a small feast of observation. John Gill weighs the timing: she had stowed them there before the messengers came, though he reports Abarbinel's view that it was after they were gone, when she moved the men from their first hiding-place to the roof, where searchers would least think to look. Adam Clarke likewise supposes the housetop concealment followed an earlier, hastier one (v.4), and dwells on the flax itself: whether flax or hemp, the Hebrew shows it was 'in its rough, unmanufactured state,' and since flax in that country ripens at the end of March or beginning of April, Rahab's crop 'might have been recently pulled, and was now drying on the roof of her house.' He adds the setting every eastern traveller knew: the flat roofs of the east, on which men walk, converse, and sleep, and for which the law of the battlement was given (Deut. 22:8).

Charles Ellicott turns the same detail into quiet evidence for the narrative's truthfulness: flax and barley are both early crops (Exod. 9:31), and the first month — the very season of the crossing (Josh. 4:19) — was the time of barley harvest. The drying flax is exactly what should be on a Jericho roof at Passover season. John Calvin presses the point furthest: had the season not been known from other sources, 'the mode of concealment to which Rahab resorted would have gone far to fix it,' for flax sown at the end of September was pulled at the end of March or beginning of April, 'which accordingly was the period when the Israelites began to move their camp.' And he adds the practical inference: had her measures of concealment not been well taken before the king's command arrived, 'there would have been no room for denial; much less would she have dared to lie so coolly.'

Matthew Henry alone draws a portrait from the flax. These were stalks 'which she herself had lain in order upon the roof to dry in the sun,' ready for beating and the wheel; here, he says, is one of the good characters of the virtuous woman — she 'sought wool and flax, and wrought willingly with her hands' (Prov. 31:13) — 'from this instance of her honest industry one would hope that, whatever she had been formerly, she was not now a harlot.' So the witnesses find in one homely image three things at once: a date for the campaign, a shelter for the spies, and a hint that grace had already set a once-disordered life to honest work.
7:
John Gill catches the narrator's irony in three words: the men pursued after them — 'as they thought.' John Wesley fills in the geography and the procedure: the pursuers took the way to the 'fords,' the known passages of Jordan where fugitives must cross, and the gate was shut behind them 'to prevent the escape of the spies, if peradventure Rahab was mistaken, and they yet lurked' within. The trap, in other words, was competently laid; every exit was covered except the one no one imagined — a window in a harlot's house upon the wall.

John Calvin reads the pursuit theologically from both ends. That the officers swallowed Rahab's story whole — 'their great credulity shows that God had blinded them.' Yet the spies' deliverance did not come as ease but as a tightening of the vise: with the gates shut, 'the city like a prison excluded the hope of escape,' and a new course of anxiety intervened. Calvin will not let us imagine the two men serene: since the history was written on their own report, they knew search was made for them; that they were still awake proves 'they were in anxiety and alarm,' and God, 'for the purpose of magnifying his grace, purposely exposed them to a succession of dangers,' arousing them again and again to call upon him. Deliverance, on this reading, is not the absence of extremity but the experience of being carried through it.

Against the trembling spies Calvin sets the extraordinary composure of their hostess, who 'bargains with so much presence of mind, and so calmly, for her own safety and that of her family.' In that firmness 'her faith, which is elsewhere commended, appears conspicuous,' for on merely human principles she would never have braved the fury of king and people to become the protector of 'guests half dead with terror.' Many, he notes, 'think there is something ridiculous in the eulogium' bestowed on her in Jas. 2:25 and Heb. 11:31; but if the tree is known by its fruits, 'we here see no ordinary effects, which are just so many evidences of faith.' The witnesses thus leave the verse poised on its own contrast: the king's men galloping toward the fords in God-sent blindness, the men of God wide awake in fear behind a barred gate, and between them a Canaanite woman whose calm is the chapter's clearest exhibition of what believing actually looks like under pressure.
8:
The witnesses note the hour and the place before the speech. John Gill, following Kimchi and Abarbinel, reads 'before they were laid down' as before they had fallen asleep; Keil and Delitzsch picture the scene exactly: as soon as the officers had left, Rahab went up to the spies, who were about to sleep on the roof itself, 'a thing of frequent occurrence in the East in summer time,' and there 'confessed to them all that she believed and knew.' The flat roof of a Canaanite house becomes, for one night, the unlikeliest confessional in Scripture.

Matthew Henry frames what follows as a treaty scene: 'the matter is here settled between Rahab and the spies respecting the service she was now to do for them, and the favour they were afterwards to show to her. She secures them on condition that they should secure her.' But he is emphatic that she gives before she asks. Coming up to men 'perhaps somewhat dismayed at the peril they apprehended themselves in... and scarcely recovered from the fright,' she has 'that to say to them which will give them abundant satisfaction' — intelligence of Canaan's inward collapse that no reconnaissance could have gathered. 'This was what they came for, and it was worth coming for.' If she kept a public house, Henry adds shrewdly, she was placed to know 'the sense of various companies and of travellers'; the spies could have learned the state of the country's heart no better way. And what she reports is nothing less than the fulfilment of a promise: God had said he would lay the fear and dread of Israel upon all the land (Deut. 11:25), and here is a Canaanite testifying it is done.

Keil and Delitzsch hear an older song behind her words: the description of the Canaanites' despair in vv.9–11 is drawn, expression by expression, from Exod. 15:15–16 — 'to show that what Moses and the Israelites had sung after crossing the Red Sea was now fulfilled,' as the Lord had pledged (Exod. 23:27; Deut. 2:25). What Israel sang in poetry forty years earlier, Rahab now reports as fact. John Wesley adds only that the men had lain down 'to sleep as they intended' — and were hindered; the interruption was worth more than the rest. The witnesses together read the verse as the hinge of the chapter: before this night the spies are fugitives receiving charity; after Rahab's confession they are ambassadors receiving a Canaanite's surrender in advance, and the mission's true report is already dictated to them on the roof among the flax.
9:
'I know that the LORD hath given you the land.' Keil and Delitzsch hear in Rahab's opening sentence the vocabulary of Israel's own worship: her terms — the terror fallen, the inhabitants melting away — are borrowed from the song at the sea (Exod. 15:15–16), and they mark the moment when the Lord's pledges to Moses (Exod. 23:27; Deut. 2:25; Deut. 11:25) passed from promise into report. A Canaanite is the first witness that the fear of Israel has done its appointed work.

But how did she come to such certainty? Here the witnesses part company. Adam Clarke takes the sober naturalistic line: 'It is likely she had this only from conjecture, having heard of their successes against the Amorites, their prodigious numbers, and seeing the state of terror and dismay to which the inhabitants of her own land were reduced.' A shrewd woman reading the times could infer that Jericho's cause was lost. Matthew Henry answers that inference alone will not carry the weight of her 'I know': the king of Jericho 'had heard as much as she had of the great things God had done for Israel, yet he cannot infer thence that the Lord had given them this land, but resolves to hold it out against them to the last extremity; for the most powerful means of conviction will not of themselves attain the end without divine grace.' By that grace Rahab, who had only heard of the wonders, 'speaks with more assurance of the truth of the promise made to the fathers than all the elders of Israel had done who were eye-witnesses,' many of whom perished through unbelief. Blessed are those that have not seen, and yet have believed.

John Calvin sides decisively with Henry and deepens the point. Profane writers, he observes, also speak of panic terrors sent by heaven, but 'while these writers prate like parrots, Rahab declaring in sincerity of heart that God has destined the land for the children of Israel... claims for him a supreme rule over the hearts of men, a rule which the pride of the world denies.' Conquerors credit their own valour; pagans made vows to Pavor and to Jupiter Stator; Rahab alone reads the dismay of nations as the hand of God and 'infers that the terror which the children of Israel have inspired is a presage of victory, because they fight under God as their Leader.' And in the same common fear, Calvin notes, faith and unbelief are distinguished: she was afraid like all her people, but she alone concluded that the only remedy was to yield humbly to the God who was giving the land — while her countrymen, equally terrified, hardened themselves to resist. John Gill simply marks the address 'unto the men,' the two spies: her creed is spoken, characteristically, not into the air but to the people of God.
10:
Rahab names her evidence: the Red Sea dried up at the Exodus, and Sihon and Og utterly destroyed beyond Jordan. John Gill pauses over the first item's age — the sea was parted 'forty years ago,' yet it was 'heard of and remembered' still in Jericho. Matthew Henry turns that persistence into doxology: the deliverance from Egypt, 'a great way off, and forty years ago,' was 'remembered and talked of afresh in Jericho, to the amazement of every body' — see how God 'makes his wonderful works to be remembered' (Ps. 111:4), so that men speak of the might of his terrible acts (Ps. 145:6). Forty years of desert wandering had not staled the story; it was waiting in Canaan's memory for Israel's return.

John Calvin analyses why this particular pair of proofs carried such force, and grades them. The victories over the Amorite kings 'rested only on conjecture' as testimonies of divine favour — armies do, after all, sometimes win battles. But the passage of the sea 'was a full and irrefragable proof, as much so as if God had stretched forth his hand from heaven,' for water 'would never have changed its nature and become piled up in solid heaps, had not God, the author of nature, so ordered.' The transmutation of the element showed beyond doubt that the God of Israel was warring for his people, and so 'all minds were seized with a conviction that in the expedition of the Israelitish people God was principal leader; hence their terror and consternation.'

Yet Calvin will not let the common terror pass for common faith. The nations, he suspects, 'were deceived by some vain imagination that the God of Israel had proved superior in the contest to the gods of Egypt,' as the poets feign patron deities warring for their favourites — pagan minds could absorb the Red Sea into polytheism and remain pagan. 'But the faith of Rahab takes a higher flight': to the God of Israel alone she will ascribe 'supreme power and eternity,' the true attributes of Jehovah. 'We see, then, how in a case where all received the same intelligence, she, in the application of it, went far beyond her countrymen.' The witnesses thus read the verse as a study in hearing: one report, published through a whole land, breeding in most only dread and defiance, and in one woman the beginnings of a confession that two apostles would still be honouring fourteen centuries later. The difference lay not in the news but in the heart that received it.
11:
The witnesses first weigh the idiom. 'Our hearts did melt' — John Wesley glosses simply, 'were dissolved, lost all courage'; Keil and Delitzsch observe that this is how Hebrew depicts utter despair, as when Israel's own heart later 'melted, and became as water' at Ai (Josh. 7:5), and that 'neither did there remain any more spirit in any man' means all strength of mind for acting was gone (elsewhere the same phrase describes sheer astonishment, 1 Kgs. 10:5). John Gill ties the collapse especially to the fate of the Amorite kings, whose peoples were utterly destroyed and their lands possessed.

Then comes the confession that crowns the speech: 'the LORD your God, he is God in heaven above, and in earth beneath.' Adam Clarke calls it 'amazingly full,' arguing 'considerable light and information' — in effect an owning of omnipotence and omnipresence — and asks where a woman of Jericho got such theology. He offers three answers without choosing: a general knowledge of the true God still diffused in the earth, though crusted with idolatry; instruction from the spies, with whom she had conversed; or 'a supernatural influence of God upon her own soul.' His resolution is pastoral: 'she probably made a better use of the light she had received than the rest of her countrymen, and God increased that light.' Keil and Delitzsch add the canonical echo: her words attain the very confession to which Israel itself was to be brought by its miracles (Deut. 4:39) — though her faith, they caution, still 'remained far behind' what Moses demanded of Israel.

John Calvin supplies both the celebration and the same caution, in his own proportions. 'Here the image of Rahab's faith appears, as if reflected in a mirror, when casting down all idols she ascribes the government of heaven and earth to the God of Israel alone' — a repudiation of every pagan fiction that portions out the world among deities. To those 'proud and disdainful men' who sneer at the apostles for enrolling her among the faithful, he answers: consider what it is 'to distinguish the one true God from all fictitious deities, and at the same time so to extol his power as to declare that the whole world is governed at his pleasure.' She speaks not hesitatingly but absolutely. And yet he freely grants that her faith 'was not fully developed — nay, only a germ of piety which, as yet, would have been insufficient for her eternal salvation'; but in surrendering to God's power 'she gives a proof of her election, and from that seed a faith was germinating which afterwards attained its full growth.' Matthew Henry gathers it up: heaven is not above his power, nor earth below his cognizance — and perhaps there was not found so great faith, no, not in Israel, as in this woman of Canaan.
12:
Confession now turns to covenant: 'swear unto me by the LORD.' John Gill notes that she asked for an oath precisely because it was 'a religious action, and done by men that feared the Lord, she knew it would be binding upon them' — and cites the Targum's paraphrase, 'swear to me by the Word of the Lord.' Adam Clarke finds here further proof of her instruction in Israel's faith: 'she acknowledged the true God by his essential character Jehovah; and knew that an oath in his name was the deepest and most solemn obligation' a Hebrew could assume (Deut. 6:13). Keil and Delitzsch settle the puzzle of the 'true token' she requests: it was no material pledge but 'a sign of truth' consisting 'in nothing but the solemn oath' itself, which per v.14 the spies actually gave.

John Calvin reads the request as faith's boldest act yet. Rahab 'places the sons of Abraham in sure possession of the land of Canaan, founding on no other argument than her having heard that it was divinely promised to them' — she did not suppose God was favouring lawless intruders, but that he had assigned them the dominion. In her, therefore, we behold that property of faith which the apostle calls the vision 'of things not appearing' (Heb. 11:1): dwelling in a fortified city, 'she commits her life to her terrified guests, just as if they had already gained possession of the land, and had full power to save or destroy as they pleased. This voluntary surrender was, in fact, the very same as embracing the promise of God.' Why then exact an oath at all? Because, Calvin answers with a soldier's realism, 'often, in the storming of cities, the heat and tumult of the struggle shook off the remembrance of duty.' And her plea for her father's house shows 'the kindliness of her disposition,' where many are so devoted to themselves that they would ransom their own lives at their parents' expense.

Matthew Henry sets her petition beside Noah's ark: foreseeing her country's fall, she 'in the belief of that bespoke in time the favour of the conquerors,' as Noah, moved with fear, prepared to the saving of his house (Heb. 11:7) — so all who believe the coming ruin will flee betimes to the mercy of the Judge. He also marks her modesty: with the spies wholly at her mercy, she might have made her own terms, yet 'she does not demand any preferment by way of reward,' only life for herself and hers. John Wesley supplies the sequel the verse cannot yet see: 'this is all that she asks. But God did for her more than she could ask or think. She was afterwards advanced to be a princess in Israel, the wife of Salmon and one of the ancestors of Christ' (Matt. 1:5). She bargained for survival and was written into the genealogy of the Saviour.
13:
Rahab's list — father, mother, brethren, sisters, and all that they have — is read closely by every witness. John Gill observes what is missing: 'she makes no mention of any husband or children she had, as harlots seldom have,' and cites Abarbinel's remark that her father's house stands 'in the room of an husband,' her brethren and sisters 'in the room of children' — for Gill a quiet confirmation of her character as the text plainly gives it. John Wesley and Keil and Delitzsch extend the list downward: 'all that they have' means the children and families of her brothers and sisters, as the fulfilment shows (Josh. 6:23). Adam Clarke explains the urgency behind the petition: 'she had learned, either from the spies or otherwise, that all the inhabitants of the land were doomed to destruction, and therefore she obliges them to enter into a covenant with her' for her whole household — she is negotiating inside a sentence of death she believes to be just.

Charles Ellicott draws from the same list a defence of the woman herself. 'Whatever Rahab may have been herself, her acknowledgment of all her family is observable. She was in no way separated or degraded from their society.' Set her beside what Moses records of Canaanite society at large (Lev. 18:24–28; Lev. 20:22–23), and 'we may reasonably conclude Rahab to have been morally not inferior to her countrymen as they were then, but rather their superior.' He reaches for the gospel parallel: the publicans and harlots were not the worst members of the 'evil and adulterous generation' to whom the Word came — they believed John the Baptist and were among the most constant hearers of the true Joshua (Matt. 21:32; Luke 15:1).

Matthew Henry turns the family plea into a pattern for believers. Her provision for parents and siblings 'is a laudable instance of natural affection, and an intimation to us in like manner to do all we can for the salvation of the souls of those that are dear to us, and, with ourselves, to bring them, if possible, into the bond of the covenant.' Though herself now a householder, 'she retained a due concern' for the family she came from. The witnesses thus converge from different directions on one portrait: a woman whom grace found in a compromised life, whose first instinct on believing the judgment to come was not to escape alone but to gather everyone she loved inside the covenant she had just secured. Faith, they agree, is here already doing what faith always does — turning outward, pleading for others, deliver our lives from death.
14:
'Our life for yours.' Adam Clarke hears in the spies' answer the old form of suretyship: 'May our life be destroyed if we suffer yours to be injured! This is what was anciently called in our country pledging — staking a man's life for that of his neighbour or friend.' John Calvin refuses the softer paraphrase 'we will pledge our lives' as too weak: the men 'imprecate death upon themselves' and 'constitute themselves a kind of expiatory victims, if any evil befalls Rahab through their negligence' — the liability extending, he insists, to her parents, brothers, and sisters. And here Calvin locates the whole sanctity of an oath: 'though its violation may escape with impunity, so far as men are concerned, yet God having been interposed as a witness, will take account of the perfidy.' To 'deal kindly and truly,' he adds, is Hebrew for performing the office of humanity 'faithfully, sincerely, and firmly.'

The condition attached — 'if ye utter not this our business' — divides interpreters less than expected, because Calvin has already reframed it. It was inserted, he argues, 'not on account of distrust, as is usually expounded, but only to put Rahab more upon her guard, on her own account': a woman boasting of her security might betray herself to the king before Israel could protect her, so 'the warning was given in good faith, and flowed from pure good will.' John Wesley reads the same clause as guarding the other flank too — 'lest others under this pretence, should secure themselves' — and admires 'both their piety and prudence in managing their oath with so much circumspection, that neither their own consciences might be ensnared, nor the publick justice obstructed.'

Matthew Henry lingers over the promise's wording. 'We will deal kindly and truly with thee' — not only kind in promising but true in performing, 'and not only true in performing just what we promise, but kind in out-doing thy demands.' The goodness of God himself is expressed by his kindness and truth (Ps. 117:2), 'and in both these we must be followers of him.' He also answers the legal scruple: the spies, as public persons, pawned 'the public faith and the credit of their nation,' interesting all Israel in the engagement; and the command to make no covenant with the Canaanites (Deut. 7:2) 'did not forbid them to take under their protection a particular person, that had heartily come into their interests and had done them real kindnesses. The law of gratitude is one of the laws of nature.' John Gill simply identifies the speakers, the two spies — but the witnesses together have already made the point: two hunted men on a Canaanite roof, swearing by the name of the LORD, bind a whole nation's honour to one believing family, and heaven itself stands witness to the bond.
15:
The escape itself is quickly told — a cord, a window, a house upon the wall — and the witnesses busy themselves with its mechanics and its placement. Adam Clarke raises the question of order: 'the natural place of this verse is after the first clause of ' v.21, 'for it is certain that she did not let them down... till all those circumstances marked from ' vv.16–20 ' inclusive had taken place.' The narrator, that is, states the escape first and then rehearses the negotiation that preceded it. John Gill and John Wesley prefer the same solution from the other side, reading the following verses as what she 'had said' before the descent — it being improbable, as Wesley argues at v.17, that such secret and weighty things were shouted down a city wall.

On the house itself the witnesses agree with unusual warmth. Her house 'was upon the town wall, and she dwelt upon the wall': Clarke explains that either the city wall formed part of the house or the house was built against it, its battlements rising above the rampart 'with a window that looked out to the country'; since the gates were shut, 'there was no way for the spies to escape but through this window.' Charles Ellicott adds, 'Happily for the two spies. Perhaps, indeed, they selected it for this reason, as it enabled them to leave the town without passing the gate.' Wesley conjectures that her own dwelling was on the wall while the rest of the house served her guests — which would explain how the transaction stayed private. Gill, ever concrete, notes that the window must have been large and the cord strong, and that in so great a secrecy she likely trusted no domestic with the work; he also reports, without endorsing, Jarchi's sour fancy that it was the same cord and window by which her paramours used to come and go — the old life's apparatus turned to the service of God's people.

The scriptural echo is unanimous: so Paul, when Damascus was watched and its gates guarded, was let down by the wall in a basket (2 Cor. 11:33) — Matthew Henry draws the parallel expressly. The people of God, the witnesses imply, have more than once owed their lives to an irregular exit: when the gate is barred by the powers of this world, providence keeps a window. And it is Rahab's window — her situation, her cord, her coolness — through which the whole mission, and with it the good report to Israel, is finally delivered.
16:
Now the hostess turns tactician: 'Get you to the mountain... hide yourselves there three days.' John Gill identifies the refuge with the mountain later called Quarantania, citing Dr. Shaw's account of the commanding prospect from its top and Josephus's description of a barren height hanging over the city — near enough to reach in the dark, wild enough to swallow two men. Charles Ellicott adds the wry New Testament footnote: 'the mountains between Jerusalem and Jericho have often been a refuge for worse characters than Joshua's two spies' (Luke 10:30). Her counsel inverts every natural instinct — the fugitives must flee away from the fords, away from home, into the hills above the city hunting them — and that is exactly why it works: the pursuers, as John Wesley notes, beat the road to Jordan while the spies sat above their heads.

The details are weighed one by one. Adam Clarke explains the discipline the advice implied: 'they were to travel by night, and hide themselves in the day-time; otherwise they might have been discovered.' Wesley computes the 'three days' by the Hebrew inclusive reckoning — 'not three whole days, but one whole day, and part of two days' — and locates caves in the encircling mountains where they might lurk. Everything in the counsel is local knowledge: she knows when the pursuers will give up, which roads they will search, how long the hue and cry will last. Matthew Henry observes that she 'directed them which way to go for their own safety, being better acquainted with the country than they were' — the protected have become the advised, and the advice is repaid at v.14 with counsel for her own safety in turn.

From this exchange the witnesses draw a common doctrine of means. Henry: 'Those that are in the way of God and their duty may expect that Providence will protect them, but this will not excuse them from taking all prudent methods for their own safety... Providence must be trusted, but not tempted.' John Calvin, commenting on this counsel, states it as a principle: 'the advice of Rahab, to turn aside into the mountain, and there remain quiet for three days, shows that there is no repugnance between faith and the precautions which provide against manifest dangers.' The men who carried Israel's promise in their mouths still crept to the mountain in fear, 'and yet that confidence which they had conceived, from the remarkable interference of God in their behalf, directed their steps, and did not allow them to lose their presence of mind.' Faith, for all these witnesses, is not the refusal of strategy but the steadiness inside it.
17:
'We will be blameless of this thine oath' — the spies begin to fence their promise, and the witnesses attend first to where and when the fencing happened. John Gill reports the common opinion that this whole discourse passed 'while in the house before she let them down, or otherwise they would have been in danger of being overheard, and so the whole affair discovered'; though he allows that, hanging under the outer face of the wall and outside the city, they might have conversed with less risk than appears. John Wesley is firmer for the pluperfect: read 'or, had said — namely, before she let them down; it being very improbable, either that she would dismiss them before the condition was agreed on; or that she would discourse with them, or they with her, about such secret and weighty things after they were let down, when others might overhear them.' Both, in effect, side with the transposition already noted at v.15: the narrative gives the escape first and the terms afterwards, but the terms were struck indoors.

On the substance, Wesley defines the key word: 'blameless — that is, free from guilt or reproach if it be violated, namely, if the following condition be not observed.' The oath is not being withdrawn but conditioned; men who have just staked their lives on its performance (v.14) are making sure its performance lies within their power. Keil and Delitzsch describe the procedure exactly: 'the spies guarded against any arbitrary interpretation and application of their oath, by imposing three conditions, on the non-fulfilment of which they would be released' — the scarlet cord in the window, the family gathered indoors, and the business kept secret, each spelled out in the verses that follow.

Matthew Henry turns the lawyer-like care into instruction. Though the spies were in haste, 'and it may be in some confusion, yet we find them very cautious in settling this agreement and the terms of it, not to bind themselves to more than was fit for them to perform.' Hence his rule: 'Covenants must be made with care, and we must swear in judgment, lest we find ourselves perplexed and entangled when it is too late... Those that will be conscientious in keeping their promises will be cautious in making them, and perhaps may insert conditions which others may think frivolous.' The witnesses find nothing grudging in the caveat: precisely because the oath was sworn by the LORD and pawned the credit of all Israel, its makers owed it exactness. A promise meant to be kept is drawn with edges; only the promise no one intends to honour can afford to be vague.
18:
The first condition is the famous one: 'thou shalt bind this line of scarlet thread in the window.' The witnesses divide cleanly over what 'this' points to. Keil and Delitzsch argue that the demonstrative 'leads to the conclusion adopted by Luther and others, that this cord is the rope' by which she was about to lower them (v.15), 'as no other word had been mentioned to which they could refer,' and nothing is said of a token being handed over; and they add the symbolic overtone that crimson, 'as the colour of vigorous life, made this cord an expressive sign of the preservation of Rahab's life and the lives of her relations.' John Wesley agrees: probably the same line with which she was about to let them down, hung out at once so the soldiers might know it. Charles Ellicott answers with philology and common sense together: the scarlet line and the lowering-cord 'are not the same thing, but described by different words in the original,' and 'it would have been preposterous to require Rahab to display in her window the means by which the spies had escaped — it would at once have declared the tale to all beholders, the very thing Rahab was pledged not to do.' He suggests instead that the thread and the flax-stalks on the roof 'were probably parts of the same business,' so that the token would look like nothing unusual, 'although it would be a sufficient token to those who were in the secret.' Adam Clarke takes a third, practical view: a piece of scarlet cloth, hung from the window 'by way of flag' when the city was taken.

The condition's other half — 'thou shalt bring thy father, and thy mother, and thy brethren, and all thy father's household, home unto thee' — is for Matthew Henry the heart of the arrangement: the sign was to mark the house so that no soldier, 'how hot and eager soever he was in military executions, might offer any violence' to it, and only those gathered within its walls could share its immunity, as Josh. 6:23 shows was honoured to the letter.

On the typology the witnesses grow bold where they had been cautious. Henry: 'this was like the blood sprinkled upon the door-post, which secured the first-born from the destroying angel, and, being of the same colour, some allude to this also to represent the safety of believers under the protection of the blood of Christ sprinkled on the conscience.' And he adds a homelier providence: 'the same cord that she made use of for the preservation of these Israelites was to be made use of for her preservation. What we serve and honour God with we may expect he will bless and make comfortable to us.' Whatever the thread had been — escape-rope, flax-ware, or flag — the witnesses agree on what it became: the one scarlet mark in a doomed city by which mercy would know where to stop.
19:
The second condition is stated in the language of the law-court. Keil and Delitzsch identify the idiom: 'his blood shall be upon his head' answers to the legal formula 'his blood be upon him' (Lev. 20:9) — whoever left the house and fell by an Israelite sword would 'bear his death as his own fault,' while for anyone within, 'his blood should fall upon the spies' head' if any hand touched him. John Wesley translates the two clauses into plain accountability: outside, 'the blame of his death shall rest wholly upon himself, as being occasioned by his own neglect of the means of safety'; inside, 'we are willing to bear the sin, and shame, and punishment of it.' The oath thus distributes responsibility with perfect symmetry — Rahab's family must trust the sign enough to stay under it; Israel must honour the sign at the cost of its own guilt if it fails.

Charles Ellicott hears in the house-bound condition an unmistakable echo: at the first Passover Israel was commanded to strike the lintel with blood 'and none of you shall go out at the door of his house until the morning' (Exod. 12:22). 'What the blood was to the houses of Israel in Egypt, that the scarlet line in the window was to the house of Rahab. Both alike prefigured “the precious blood of Christ.”' In both cases, deliverance is not a private quality carried in the person but a place appointed by promise: safety lies where the token is, and nowhere else that night.

Matthew Henry expounds why the proviso was no formality. Rahab's kindred 'could not be distinguished any other way than by being in her distinguished house; should they mingle with their neighbours, there was no remedy, but the sword would devour one as well as another.' And he presses the figure to its evangelical conclusion: if they would not perish with them that believed not, they must 'thus far believe the certainty and severity of the ruin coming upon their city as to retire into a place made safe by promise, as Noah into the ark and Lot into Zoar,' saving themselves from an untoward generation by separating from it. The application follows: 'those who are added to the church that they may be saved must keep close to the society of the faithful,' and having escaped the world's corruption must not be entangled again. John Gill keeps the scene concrete — the clause looks to the day when Israel is actually in the city — but the witnesses' joint reading is already complete: one house under one scarlet sign, and every life in Jericho sorted, that day, by a threshold.
20:
The third condition closes the treaty where it began: 'if thou utter this our business, then we will be quit of thine oath.' Keil and Delitzsch observe that this 'is simply a repetition of the principal condition laid down at the very outset' (v.14) — the whole covenant is bracketed by secrecy, first exacted when the spies pledged their lives and now restated as the final seal before the parties separate. John Gill spells out the mischief the clause forestalls: were the agreement published, others might 'hang out scarlet threads' of their own, or crowd into her house for shelter, and the token would be confounded — the sign only saves while it is singular.

Adam Clarke reads the stipulation as plain political prudence with a shrewd psychological edge: 'It was prudent to make her life depend on her secresy; had it been otherwise she might have been tempted to give information, not only concerning the spies, but concerning the designs of the Israelites. But her life being at stake, added to every other motive, she kept the secret for the sake of her own personal safety and that of all her relatives.' The spies, on this reading, aligned her interest with their own so exactly that fidelity became her safest course as well as her sworn one. John Calvin, from his exposition of the earlier clause, had already turned the same observation in a kindlier direction: the condition was inserted 'not on account of distrust... but only to put Rahab more upon her guard, on her own account,' a warning 'given in good faith' which 'flowed from pure good will,' since a careless word about the compact could have cost her her head for treason before Israel ever crossed the river. The two readings are not rivals: the clause at once protected the sign, protected the woman, and protected the swearers.

Matthew Henry distils the lesson into a maxim: if she should betray the business when they were gone, or publish the agreement so that others tied scarlet lines in their windows, the men would be clear of the oath — for 'those are unworthy of the secret of the Lord that know not how to keep it to themselves when there is occasion.' Discretion, in his hands, becomes almost a grace of the covenant: the same faith that dares to hide spies must also know how to hold its tongue. So the treaty ends as covenants of grace do in every age — sworn in God's name, hedged with conditions that are themselves mercies, and committed to the keeping of a believer whose silence is now part of her obedience.
21:
Rahab's answer is a model of covenant consent: 'According unto your words, so be it.' John Gill paraphrases the exchange as a completed contract — she agreed that if the conditions failed the men were quit of their oath, and expected that if they were kept the oath would be kept too. Nothing is renegotiated, nothing protested; the woman who bargained so boldly for her family's lives (vv.12–13) accepts without demur every restriction laid on the promise, because the restrictions themselves were within her power to fulfil. Then she sent them away, and they departed: and she bound the scarlet line in the window.

That last clause raises the one question the witnesses debate: when? John Wesley takes the narrative order at face value and gives reasons for it: she bound the line 'forthwith, partly that the spies might see it hung out before their departure, and so the better know it at some distance; partly lest some accident might occasion a neglect about it' — prompt obedience as insurance against forgetfulness, and a visible ratification of the treaty while its other parties could still witness it. Keil and Delitzsch demur: 'It is not to be supposed that she did this at once, but merely as soon as it was necessary. It is mentioned here for the purpose of bringing the subject to a close' — a common habit of Hebrew narrative, which finishes a topic before turning to the next. A scarlet cord dangling from the wall for weeks, they imply, might have invited the very questions the secrecy clause forbade; and Adam Clarke's observation at v.15, that the letting down itself belongs after the first clause of this verse, shows how freely the narrator has arranged these closing details by theme rather than by clock.

Small as it is, the question lets each side show its character: Wesley's reading prizes the eagerness of faith, which performs its part of the covenant before the ink is dry; the critical reading prizes the coherence of the story, in which a wise woman would not advertise her secret a day sooner than she must. On the substance all agree, and it is the substance that preaches: the token of her deliverance was bound in the window by her own hands, in simple reliance on a promise whose fulfilment was still invisible. Like the faith praised in Heb. 11:31, her obedience did not wait to see Israel cross the Jordan. The scarlet line was tied while Jericho still stood, its walls unbreached and its king still hunting spies — which is to say, it was tied by faith.
22:
The spies obey their hostess to the letter: to the mountain, three days, until the pursuers return. Keil and Delitzsch supply the terrain: the refuge was 'probably the range on the northern side of Jericho, which afterwards received the name of Quarantana... a wall of rock rising almost precipitously from the plain to the height of 1200 or 1500 feet, and full of grottoes and caves on the eastern side' — 'well adapted for a place of concealment,' and the nearest cover to the city. John Gill pictures them climbing to the top and lying hid in some cave, and John Wesley adds the domestic detail that they supported themselves there 'with the provisions, which Rahab had furnished them with' — her hospitality reaching beyond her own roof into the wilderness. Meanwhile the hunt spent itself exactly as she had foretold: the pursuers 'sought them throughout all the way,' the road to Jordan and the places near it, Wesley notes, 'but not in the mountains.'

The chronology gets its due. Charles Ellicott reads the 'three days' as reckoned 'from the commencement of their mission, according to the usual inclusive reckoning of the Old Testament' — so that the whole reconnaissance fits inside the three days Joshua had announced at the camp; Wesley had likewise computed at v.16 one whole day and parts of two. The apparently loose numbers of the narrative knit, on inspection, into a tight timetable.

Matthew Henry reads the episode for its meaning to Israel. The spies' safe return 'was itself an encouragement to Joshua, and a token for good': that God provided them 'so good a friend as Rahab was in an enemy's country,' and brought them back in peace notwithstanding the rage of the king and the eagerness of the pursuers, 'was such an instance of God's great care concerning them for Israel's sake as might assure the people of the divine guidance and care they were under.' And he draws the a fortiori that turns a scouting report into a sermon: 'He that so wonderfully protected their scouts would preserve their men of war, and cover their heads in the day of battle.' The three silent days in the caves of Quarantana, on this reading, were not dead time but demonstration — proof in miniature, before the Jordan was ever crossed, that the God who had promised the land could keep two men alive in the middle of it against a kingdom's whole police. What he did for the two he would do for the twelve tribes.
23:
The mission closes with four brisk verbs — returned, descended, passed over, came — and the witnesses fill in the itinerary. Adam Clarke reconstructs the timetable: 'having concealed themselves in the mountains that night, all the next day, and the night ensuing, on the third day they returned to Joshua.' John Gill cites Kimchi's finer reckoning, that the descent from the mountain fell 'on the third day of their being sent, which was the second day of the three days Joshua made mention of when he said, within three days' — the spies' adventure slotting neatly into the campaign clock of the first chapter. How they crossed the Jordan, swollen in harvest flood, the text leaves as silent as it left their outward passage; they 'passed over' as men do whom God is bringing home.

Keil and Delitzsch mark the completeness of the debriefing: they came to Joshua 'and told him all things that befell them' — 'informed Joshua of all that had befallen them, and all that they had heard.' Nothing edited, nothing withheld: the narrow escape, the woman, the oath that now bound Israel's honour, and the intelligence of a country already melting in fear. The report of what befell them and the report of what they heard belong together, for in this mission the dangers were themselves data — every hair's-breadth deliverance was evidence of the same divine favour that Rahab's confession asserted.

Matthew Henry reads the temper of the returning men against the dark precedent that shadows every espionage story in Israel. 'Had they been disposed to discourage the people, as the evil spies did that Moses sent, they might have told them what they had observed of the height and strength of the walls of Jericho, and the extraordinary vigilance of the king of Jericho, and how narrowly they escaped out of his hands.' The raw material of a faithless report was all there — fortifications, alert enemies, a manhunt survived by inches. 'But they were of another spirit, and, depending themselves upon the divine promise, they animated Joshua likewise.' The same facts that unbelief would have arranged into an argument for despair, faith arranged into an argument for advance. The witnesses leave the verse as a quiet study in testimony: what a messenger reports is determined less by what he has seen than by what he believes about the God who sent him — and these two, unlike their fathers' generation, came back believing.
24:
'Truly the LORD hath delivered into our hands all the land.' Adam Clarke sets the sentence against its shadow: 'How different was this report from that brought by the spies on a former occasion!' — and Jamieson-Fausset-Brown supply the contrast's history, recalling that the earlier espionage 'emanated from the people through unbelief' and 'proved disastrous to them, only through their own sin and folly.' This time two men had seen the same kind of walls and the same kind of enemy, and returned with a conclusion of faith. John Calvin makes their words the vindication of their commander's choice: 'this passage shows that Joshua was not mistaken in selecting his spies; for their language proves them to have been right-hearted men possessed of rare integrity.' Others, scarcely recovered from such terror, 'would have disturbed the whole camp,' but these exhort Joshua and the people to go boldly forward. And Calvin marks the tenderness in the arrangement: the bare promise ought to have sufficed, 'yet the Lord is so very indulgent to their weakness, that, for the sake of removing all doubt, he confirms what he had promised by experience.' They argue exactly as Rahab had argued — the land is given, because its inhabitants faint.

Matthew Henry converts the intelligence into doctrine: the Canaanites 'have neither wisdom to yield nor courage to fight,' and 'sinners' frights are sometimes sure presages of their fall'; the terrors that dismay the ungodly without bringing them to repentance 'are fearful forebodings of approaching destruction. But grace yet abounds to the chief of sinners' — Rahab standing in the report itself as proof that even in a doomed city faith can find the scarlet line. Clarke closes the chapter with two appendices. First, the lie once more: did Rahab lie? 'She certainly did'; she was rewarded 'for her hospitality and faith, not for her lie.' Against Tasso's celebrated 'magnanima menzogna' — the 'lie divinely fair' told to save a people — he sets the sterner ancients and George Herbert's couplet, 'Dare to be true! nothing can need a lie; the fault that needs it most grows two thereby.' The religion of Christ 'is one eternal system of truth, and can neither be served by a lie nor admit one.'

His second appendix gathers the whole chapter's practical divinity. Though the hand of God was in everything, and Israel knew the land would be won by his might alone, 'yet they were as fully convinced that if they did not use the counsel, prudence, and strength which they had received from him, they should not succeed.' Depending on divine direction, they exercised their own prudence; trusting divine power, they put forth their own strength; 'and thus they were workers together with him, and did not receive the grace of God in vain.' On that the witnesses close in full agreement, for it is the chapter in miniature: a general who prays and also reconnoitres, spies who believe and also hide three days in a cave, a convert who confesses the God of heaven and earth and also ties a cord in her window — grace sovereign throughout, and not one means neglected.
